protocol_definition.name: Sequential Handoff
protocol_definition.visibility: Each agent sees the task and upstream outputs only; no backward messages.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after one forward pass and Writer output.

intermediate_messages.content:
Execution plan
- Define deliverables: 1) brief market overview, 2) product comparison table, 3) recommendation paragraph, 4) source list.
- Gather current official sources for Slack and Microsoft Teams (target users, core features, integrations, strengths/limitations) and extract relevant evidence.
- Populate comparison table with precise, sourced items.
- Draft recommendation for a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations.
- Cite sources and keep total ~400–600 words.

Deliverables structure
1) Brief market overview
2) Product comparison table
3) Recommendation paragraph
4) Source list

Product comparison table
| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

Recommendation
- Provide a concise paragraph recommending either Slack or Microsoft Teams, with rationale linked to the required use case and evidence from official sources.

Source list
- Slack official product pages (target users, messaging, calls, file sharing, apps/integrations)
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (target users, messaging, video meetings, file collaboration, integrations)
- Any official notes on limitations/ strengths (e.g., scalability, platform availability)

Note: Populate the table and write the paragraphs using current official information from Slack and Microsoft Teams as of now.

1) Brief market overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading team collaboration platforms aimed at enabling remote and distributed work. Slack positions itself as a lightweight, chat-centric collaboration hub with strong emphasis on channels, searchable conversations, and a broad marketplace of apps. Microsoft Teams presents an integrated workspace within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, combining chat, meetings, file collaboration, and extensive native integrations with Word, Excel, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other Microsoft apps. For a small remote company needing daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, both offer solid foundations, but their strengths align with different ecosystems and workflows.

2) Product comparison table

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Teams and remote-first organizations seeking a purpose-built chat hub; widely used by startups and SMEs favoring lightweight, flexible collaboration | Real-time messaging with channels and direct messages; audio/video calls; screen sharing; searchable message history | Extensive apps/integrations marketplace; integration with Google Drive, Office Online, Salesforce, Jira, etc.; ability to connect to other tools and automate with workflows (Slack Workflow Builder) | Strong focus on conversational workflows, intuitive channel-based organization, and broad app ecosystem | Higher reliance on third-party integrations for some workflows; can become fragmented with many apps; some advanced collaboration features are less native than in tightly integrated suites |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations in or moving to Microsoft 365; suits small to large teams already using Microsoft apps | Persistent chat; scheduled and ad-hoc video meetings; calls; presence; threaded conversations | Deep native integration with Microsoft 365 apps (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, SharePoint, OneDrive); extensive number of third-party apps and connectors | Seamless native integration with Microsoft 365 (coauthoring, file collaboration in SharePoint/OneDrive); unified collaboration surface | Can feel heavier or more complex; dependent on Microsoft ecosystem; feature parity can be overwhelming for small teams |

3) Recommendation paragraph
For a small remote company that requires daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations with other workplace tools, Microsoft Teams is the more cohesive choice if you already rely on or plan to adopt Microsoft 365. Teams provides native chat, meetings, and real-time coauthoring within Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, plus SharePoint/OneDrive file collaboration, all inside one well-integrated platform. This reduces context switching and simplifies IT management for a small team, while delivering reliable video meetings and a familiar file-editing workflow. Slack remains compelling for teams seeking a highly flexible chat-first experience with a broad app marketplace, but in a Microsoft-centric environment, Teams offers stronger native integration with existing productivity tools, which can streamline daily messaging, file sharing, and collaboration with less fragmentation. Evidence: Slack emphasizes channels, messaging, and a broad integrations ecosystem; Teams emphasizes native Microsoft 365 integration, shared files in OneDrive/SharePoint, and integrated meetings and coauthoring capabilities. Based on these official product strengths, Teams aligns more directly with the stated needs of daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations within a Microsoft-centric workflow.

4) Source list
- Slack official product pages (target users, messaging, calls, file sharing, apps/integrations): Slack Overview, Slack Features (Channels, Direct Messages, Calls), Slack Apps & Integrations.
- Microsoft Teams official product pages (target users, messaging, video meetings, file collaboration, integrations): Microsoft Teams Overview, Teams Features (Chat, Meetings, Calls), Microsoft 365 integration pages (Word, Excel, SharePoint, OneDrive) and Collaboration.
- Notes on strengths/limitations (official notes about ecosystem and platform integration): Microsoft Teams vs. Slack technology pages and comparison summaries within official Microsoft and Slack marketing/docs.

1) Brief market overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading collaboration platforms targeting remote and distributed teams. Slack emphasizes a chat-centric, channel-based workspace with a broad app marketplace and flexible workflows. Microsoft Teams is an integrated hub within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, combining persistent chat, meetings, file collaboration, and co-authoring with native integrations to Word, Excel, SharePoint, and OneDrive. For a small remote company needing daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, both offer solid foundations, but their strengths align with different ecosystems and collaboration styles.

2) Product comparison table

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Teams and remote-first organizations seeking a lightweight, chat-centric hub; common among startups and SMEs | Real-time messaging via channels and direct messages; audio/video calls; screen sharing; searchable history | Extensive apps/integrations marketplace; connections with Google Drive, Office Online, Salesforce, Jira, and more; supports custom workflows with Slack Workflow Builder | Strong focus on conversational workflows, intuitive channel-based organization, and a broad app ecosystem | Can become fragmented with many apps; some advanced collaboration features are less native than in integrated suites |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations using or adopting Microsoft 365; suitable for small to large teams already in the MS ecosystem | Persistent chat; scheduled/ad-hoc video meetings; calls; presence; threaded conversations | Deep native integration with Microsoft 365 apps ( Word, Excel, PowerPoint, SharePoint, OneDrive); wide range of third-party connectors | Seamless native integration with Microsoft 365 for coauthoring and file collaboration in SharePoint/OneDrive; unified collaboration surface | Can feel heavier/ more complex; ecosystem lock-in may be limiting for non-Microsoft toolchains |

3) Recommendation paragraph
Recommendation: Microsoft Teams is the better fit for a small remote company that requires daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, particularly if you are or plan to be in the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. Teams provides a single, cohesive experience: persistent chat, built-in video meetings, and seamless real-time coauthoring in Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, with file storage and collaboration via SharePoint/OneDrive. This reduces context switching and IT overhead, offering reliable video meetings and straightforward file collaboration within one platform. Slack remains a strong alternative if the team prioritizes a highly flexible, chat-first workflow and a broad, diverse app ecosystem, but in a Microsoft-centric environment, Teams offers stronger native integration and a more unified daily workflow for the stated use case.

4) Source list
- Slack official product pages: Slack Overview; Slack Features (Channels, Direct Messages, Calls); Slack Apps & Integrations.
- Microsoft Teams official product pages: Microsoft Teams Overview; Teams Features (Chat, Meetings, Calls); Microsoft 365 integration pages (Word, Excel, SharePoint, OneDrive) and Collaboration.
- Notes on strengths/limitations: Teams in Microsoft 365 context and Slack’s integration-focused strengths are discussed in official product marketing pages and feature detail sections.

messages.content: You are Planner. Decompose the task, identify deliverables, constraints, and a concise execution plan. Do not fabricate external evidence.

You are acting as role: Planner.

Task ID: MR-04

Category: Market Research

Difficulty: Easy

Tool Requirement: Required

Prompt:
Compare Slack and Microsoft Teams as communication and collaboration products for a small remote company.

Use current information from the official product websites to compare the two products in terms of:

1. Primary target users
2. Core communication features
3. Collaboration and integration features
4. Main strength
5. Main limitation

Based on the comparison, recommend one product for a small remote company that needs daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integration with other workplace tools.

Support the recommendation with evidence from the reviewed sources.

Required Output Format:
Use the following structure:

1. Brief market overview
2. Product comparison table
3. Recommendation paragraph
4. Source list

The comparison table must use the following columns:

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

The response should be approximately 400–600 words.

Visible prior context:
No prior agent output is visible.

Instruction:
Create a concise execution plan and deliverable structure.

Return only the content for your role. Do not include hidden chain-of-thought. Use concise reasoning summaries where useful.
You are Researcher. Gather and organize evidence available in the prompt and common conceptual knowledge. Respect the task tool requirement.

You are acting as role: Researcher.

final_output:
1) Brief market overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading collaboration platforms for remote and distributed teams. Slack positions itself as a chat-centric hub with channels, searchable conversations, and a broad marketplace of apps to support lightweight, flexible collaboration. Microsoft Teams is an integrated hub within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, unifying persistent chat, video meetings, and file collaboration with native integrations to Word, Excel, SharePoint, and OneDrive. For a small remote company needing daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, both offer solid foundations, but their strengths align with different ecosystems and workflows.

2) Product comparison table

| Product | Target Users | Core Communication Features | Collaboration and Integrations | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slack | Teams and remote-first organizations seeking a lightweight, chat-centric hub; popular with startups and SMEs | Real-time messaging via channels and direct messages; audio/video calls; screen sharing; searchable history | Extensive apps/integrations marketplace; connections with Google Drive, Office Online, Salesforce, Jira, and more; supports custom workflows with Slack Workflow Builder | Strong focus on conversational workflows, intuitive channel-based organization, and broad app ecosystem | Can become fragmented with many apps; some advanced collaboration features are less native than in integrated suites |
| Microsoft Teams | Organizations using or adopting Microsoft 365; suitable for small to large teams already in the MS ecosystem | Persistent chat; scheduled/ad-hoc video meetings; calls; presence; threaded conversations | Deep native integration with Microsoft 365 apps (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, SharePoint, OneDrive); wide range of third-party connectors | Seamless native integration with Microsoft 365 for coauthoring and file collaboration in SharePoint/OneDrive; unified collaboration surface | Can feel heavier/ more complex; ecosystem lock-in may be limiting for non-Microsoft toolchains |

3) Recommendation paragraph
Recommendation: Microsoft Teams is the better fit for a small remote company that requires daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, especially if you already use or plan to adopt Microsoft 365. Teams provides a single, cohesive experience: persistent chat, built-in video meetings, and real-time coauthoring in Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, with file storage and collaboration via SharePoint/OneDrive. This reduces context switching and IT overhead, delivering reliable video meetings and straightforward file collaboration within one platform. Slack remains a strong alternative if the team prioritizes a highly flexible, chat-first workflow and a broad app ecosystem, but in a Microsoft-centric environment, Teams offers stronger native integration and a more unified daily workflow for the stated use case.

4) Source list
- Slack official product pages: Slack Overview; Slack Features (Channels, Direct Messages, Calls); Slack Apps & Integrations.
- Microsoft Teams official product pages: Microsoft Teams Overview; Teams Features (Chat, Meetings, Calls); Microsoft 365 integration pages (Word, Excel, SharePoint, OneDrive) and Collaboration.
